_comment: Tidbit content pack. The app rotates through these and falls back to them when live data is unavailable. Each entry: id (unique), category, text (full sentence), and optional season_window (date range YYYY-MM-DD..YYYY-MM-DD), weight (rotation priority 1-10, default 5), trigger (context-conditional). Target growth: 150+ entries before parent debut. Categories: nostalgia, did_you_know, ballpark, tonight_nugget, player_spotlight, wholesome, stat_nerd, opponent_history.

entries.text: October 30, 2019: Howie Kendrick rang the foul pole in the seventh inning of Game 7. The Nationals won the World Series. Still the only one.
The 2019 parade started at the Capitol and ended at Nationals Park. Stephen Strasburg held the trophy. Everyone in DC took a Monday off.
Gerardo Parra changed his walk-up song to 'Baby Shark' for his daughter in June 2019. The team caught fire and never looked back.
In 2019, the Nats swept the Cardinals in the NLCS. Anibal Sanchez carried a no-hitter into the eighth in Game 1.
Down 3-1 in the eighth, Juan Soto drove in three with a single off Josh Hader. The 2019 Wild Card came back to life right there.
Dave Martinez told them after the 19-31 start: 'Stay in the fight.' That phrase lived in the dugout the rest of the year.
Stephen Strasburg was World Series MVP — 2-0, 14 strikeouts in 14.1 IP. He earned every dollar of his next contract that month.
Patrick Corbin came out of the bullpen to win Game 7. The signed-as-a-starter, throwing-three-innings-of-relief Game 7. That kind of clutch.
Ryan Zimmerman holds the franchise record for career hits, home runs, and walk-offs. They call him Mr. National for good reason.
Anthony Rendon hit three home runs and drove in ten runs against the Mets in April 2017. Three. Ten. In one game.
Stephen Strasburg holds the Nats franchise record for strikeouts. He retired in 2024 as the Game 6 World Series MVP.
Max Scherzer struck out 300 in 2018. Nobody on the Nats had ever done it. He was 33.
Juan Soto made his MLB debut at age 19 in May 2018. He homered in his first start. The youngest Nat to ever go deep.
Bryce Harper won the 2018 Home Run Derby at Nationals Park. He hit nineteen in the final round. The crowd did not sit down.
The 2020 season was 60 games. Nationals Park sat empty. Cardboard cutouts watched from the stands. Defense of the title got weird.
The 2021 trade deadline reset the Nats — Scherzer and Trea Turner went to the Dodgers. The rebuild started that day.
August 2, 2022: Juan Soto was traded to San Diego. CJ Abrams, MacKenzie Gore, James Wood, and Robert Hassell came back. The future arrived.
The 2024 Nats overachieved. James Wood arrived. CJ Abrams broke out. The kids were ready earlier than anyone expected.
Ryan Zimmerman was the Nationals' very first draft pick in 2005, the same year the franchise moved from Montreal.
The Nationals were the Montreal Expos from 1969 to 2004. The curly W honors the original Senators, not the Expos.
The curly W on the Nats cap is a callback to the Washington Senators logo from 1911. Some traditions outlast everything.
The first regular-season Nats game was April 4, 2005, in Philadelphia. They lost 8-4. They didn't win their first home game until April 14.
The Nats won their first NL East title in 2012, 24 wins better than the prior season. Bryce Harper was 19. Stephen Strasburg was 23.
Jordan Zimmermann threw a no-hitter on the last day of the 2014 regular season — the first in Nationals/Expos history.
Max Scherzer threw two no-hitters in 2015 — the second one a few outs from a perfect game. He hit a batter on a 1-2 pitch in the ninth. One pitch from immortality.
Nationals Park sits where the Anacostia River meets the Potomac watershed. The cherry blossoms in section 137 bloom every April.
Nationals Park seats 41,313 people. The lower bowl gets sun in the afternoon; the upper deck gets the breeze.
The Red Porch in right-center field is one of the best places in MLB to watch a game. Home runs land just past the bullpens.
Nationals Park is 336 feet down the left field line, 402 to dead center, 335 to right. The right-center alley is where the wind helps.
From the upper deck along third base, you can see the Capitol dome. The architects designed it that way on purpose.
Navy Yard-Ballpark Metro station opens onto the Center Field gate. Easiest big-league commute in baseball.
The Presidents Race happens in the fourth inning. Teddy didn't win once between 2006 and 2012 — 525 races without a victory. Then he finally won.
Screech the bald eagle has been the Nats mascot since 2005. Kids who first met him at the inaugural season are bringing their own kids now.
Half-smoke from Ben's Chili Bowl is the official ballpark hot dog. President Obama waited in line for one, like everyone else.
The Nats have looked sharp lately. Let's see if they can keep it rolling.
Tough stretch. Tonight's a fresh start. That's why they play 162.
Home game tonight at Nationals Park. The grounds crew has the diamond dialed in.
On the road tonight. Different ballpark, same hope.
Early April baseball is its own thing. Cold hands, fast wind, the season finding its legs.
May baseball. The weather warms up and the standings start to mean something.
Long June evenings. First pitch and last out both still in daylight. Best month of the schedule.
July nights. Heat, humidity, fireworks weekends. Trade deadline rumblings on the horizon.
August grind. The heart of the schedule. Every game starts to feel like it has weight.
September baseball. Rosters expand, prospects get a look, every game a separate story.
James Wood is twenty-two. He hits the ball harder than almost anyone in baseball. The future started already.
CJ Abrams' sprint speed is in the 95th percentile of all of MLB. Watch his first step out of the box.
MacKenzie Gore was the third overall pick in 2017. The Nats got him in the Soto trade. The fastball is real.
Dylan Crews was the second overall pick in 2023. He hits, he runs, he throws. The complete package.
Luis García Jr. emerged as the everyday second baseman in 2024. The bat speed plays.
Keibert Ruiz is the catcher of the present and future. He's young, he hits, he frames pitches well.
Kyle Finnegan, the All-Star closer. Came up as an undrafted free agent. Now he's the guy in the ninth.
Jake Irvin: a workhorse. Eats innings. Doesn't get rattled. The kind of arm you build a rotation around.
Mitchell Parker came up faster than expected and held his own. Good story, good arm.
Gerardo Parra's daughter picked 'Baby Shark' as his walk-up song. The whole stadium learned the hand motions.
Ryan Zimmerman's foundation has raised millions for MS research, named for his mother.
Screech the bald eagle has been the Nats mascot since 2005. He turns the same age as the franchise every year.
Stephen Strasburg's retirement ceremony was perfect. The whole 2019 team came back. There wasn't a dry eye in the upper deck.
Every kid's first big-league game is the same: the green of the field, the size of the players, the smell of the popcorn. Nationals Park has been that for almost 20 years.
More than a hundred marriage proposals happen at Nationals Park every season. Right between innings, on the kiss cam, no pressure.
Mom and Dad. This app exists because watching the Nats together is one of the best things.
Nationals Park is roughly league-average for run scoring. The right-center power alley is the friendliest spot.
The NL East has been won by every team in it since 2017. Parity, finally.
James Wood's average exit velocity puts him in elite company — Judge, Stanton, Schwarber tier.
CJ Abrams was top-five in steals over the second half of 2024. The threat alone changes a game.
MacKenzie Gore's fastball spin rate is in the top 15% of MLB starters. The ball jumps on hitters more than the radar gun says.
Kyle Finnegan's save conversion rate the last two seasons is over 90%. Quietly elite.
The Nats are one of the youngest position-player groups in MLB. Most of the lineup is still inside their team-control years.
Wood, García, Abrams — three left-handed bats with 90+ exit velocity. Right-handed pitchers get the long part of the order.
Atlanta. The biggest division test, every series. The 2019 NLDS-winning Game 5 was vintage Nats.
Philly. Familiar opponent, mixed memories. Tonight's another chapter.
Citi Field has not always been kind. Then again, neither has Nats Park to them.
Miami. Indoor baseball in the summer is a strange thing. The Nats have generally fared well there.
The Dodgers and the Nats have history — the 2016 NLDS, the 2019 NLDS comeback. Different era, same names sometimes.
Interleague vs the Yankees is rare and loud. Makes you wish baseball did it more often.
Beltway Series. The drive between the parks is short. The history between the cities is long.
Red Sox in town. Different league, different vibe, fun for one weekend.
Max Scherzer threw the second no-hitter in franchise history (after Zimmermann's). It came against the Mets, October 3, 2015. He struck out 17.
Game 5 of the 2017 NLDS against the Cubs was a five-hour heartbreak. They came back and won it all two years later. Worth the wait.
Sean Doolittle was a key piece of the 2019 bullpen. He retired with eternal goodwill in DC.
Luis García Jr. — his dad played for the Nats too. They share a name and a uniform number history.
Dave Martinez took heat after the 2018 dugout incident. He kept showing up. Then 2019 happened. The right manager, the right time.
Stephen Strasburg's career postseason ERA is 1.46. Sometimes the moment is bigger than the regular season.
